summary: Kenneth documents a challenging week executing his $180/week covered call income strategy on HIMS, resulting in a 3% return ($365 profit) after being forced to hold significant capital in losing positions. The video emphasizes risk management lessons learned from blind copiers and demonstrates how assignment on underwater cash-secured puts can freeze capital and derail premium collection.
takeaways: Selling weekly covered calls carries substantial risk and requires high risk tolerance, significant capital, and experience
Do not copy trades blindly without understanding the full risk profile and position management
When assigned on cash-secured puts at a loss (bought HIMS at $52 when stock at $47), hold as unrealized loss only if technical analysis supports recovery
Use 50-day and 200-day moving average crossovers as bullish indicators for timing covered call re-entry
Capital tied up in losing positions ($20k HIMS + $5k QuantumCape) prevents execution of income strategy elsewhere
Assignment on OTM covered calls frees capital back for redeployment ($20k returned from SoFi/Rivian/Nphase assignments)
Accept that some weeks yield only 3% returns when capital is constrained by underwater long stock positions

strategy_candidates.change: Establish a maximum capital allocation rule for cash-secured puts (e.g., no more than 30% of portfolio) to preserve liquidity for ongoing covered call income generation on target positions
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth had to hold $17k-$15k across two cash-secured put contracts while executing the income strategy, which constrained his ability to sell covered calls weekly and reduced his profit to $365 (3%) instead of the target $1,250/mo; he explicitly stated this frozen capital prevented him from reaching his income goals

title: Part 12: 8/22/25 - $180 per week from HIMS fail
